1. HashEvict: A Pre-Attention KV Cache Eviction Strategy using Locality-Sensitive Hashing (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 9)
   Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) use the key-value (KV) cache to significantly accelerate inference by storing the key and value embeddings of past tokens. However, this cache consumes significant GPU memory. In this work, we introduce HashEvict, an algorithm that uses locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) to compress the KV cache. HashEvict quickly locates tokens in the cache that are cosine dissimilar to the current query token. This is achieved by computing the Hamming distance between binarized Gaussian projections of the current token query and cached token keys, with a projection length much smaller than the embedding dimension. We maintain a lightweight binary structure in GPU memory to facilitate these calculations. Unlike existing compression strategies that compute attention to determine token retention, HashEvict makes these decisions pre-attention, thereby reducing computational costs. Additionally, HashEvict is dynamic - at every decoding step, the key and value of the current token replace the embeddings of a token expected to produce the lowest attention score. We demonstrate that HashEvict can compress the KV cache by 30%-70% while maintaining high performance across reasoning, multiple-choice, long-context retrieval and summarization tasks.

2. Cache What Lasts: Token Retention for Memory-Bounded KV Cache in LLMs (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 5)
   Memory and computation remain core bottlenecks in long-horizon LLM inference due to the quadratic cost of self-attention and the ever-growing key-value (KV) cache. Existing strategies for memory-bounded inference, such as quantization, offloading, or heuristic KV eviction, either incur high orchestration costs or rely on unreliable attention-based proxies of importance. We propose TRIM-KV, a novel approach that learns each token's intrinsic importance at creation time via a lightweight retention gate. Each gate predicts a scalar retention score that decays over time, reflecting the long-term utility of the token for a specific layer and head. Tokens with low scores are evicted when the memory budget is exceeded, ensuring that the cache always contains the most critical tokens. TRIM-KV is trained efficiently through distillation from a frozen LLM combined with a capacity loss, requiring only gate fine-tuning and adding negligible inference overhead. Across mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME24), procedural generation (LongProc), conversational long-memory benchmarks (LongMemEval), and long-context understanding (LongBenchV2 and SCBench), TRIM-KV consistently outperforms strong eviction and learnable retrieval baselines, especially in low-memory regimes. Remarkably, it even surpasses full-cache models in some settings, showing that selective retention can serve as a form of regularization, suppressing noise from uninformative tokens. Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Beyond efficiency, retention scores provide insights into layer- and head-specific roles, suggesting a new path toward LLM interpretability.

3. VitaLLM: A Versatile and Tiny Accelerator for Mixed-Precision LLM Inference on Edge Devices ( 2026, cites: 0)
   We present VitaLLM, a mixed precision accelerator that enables ternary weight large language models to run efficiently on edge devices. The design combines two compute cores, a multiplier free TINT core for ternary-INT projections and a BoothFlex core that reuses a radix-4 Booth datapath for both INT8$\times$INT8 attention and ternary-INT-sustaining utilization without duplicating arrays. A predictive sparse attention mechanism employs a leading-one (LO) surrogate with a comparison-free top-$K$ selector to prune key/value (KV) fetches by roughly $1-K/M$ for $M$ cached tokens, confining exact attention to $K$ candidates. System-level integration uses head-level pipelining and an absmax-based quantization barrier to standardize cross-core interfaces and overlap nonlinear reductions with linear tiles. A 16 nm silicon prototype at 1 GHz/0.8 V achieves 72.46 tokens/s in decode and 0.88 s prefill (64 tokens) within 0.214 mm^2 and 120 KB on-chip memory, while reducing KV traffic and improving utilization in ablations. These results demonstrate practical BitNet b1.58 (3B) inference on edge-class platforms and provide a compact blueprint for future mixed-precision LLM accelerators.

1. DASH-KV: Accelerating Long-Context LLM Inference via Asymmetric KV Cache Hashing ( 2026, cites: 0)
   The quadratic computational complexity of the standard attention mechanism constitutes a fundamental bottleneck for large language models in long-context inference. While existing KV cache compression methods alleviate memory pressure, they often sacrifice generation quality and fail to address the high overhead of floating-point arithmetic. This paper introduces DASH-KV, an innovative acceleration framework that reformulates attention as approximate nearest-neighbor search via asymmetric deep hashing. Under this paradigm, we design an asymmetric encoding architecture that differentially maps queries and keys to account for their distinctions in precision and reuse characteristics. To balance efficiency and accuracy, we further introduce a dynamic mixed-precision mechanism that adaptively retains full-precision computation for critical tokens. Extensive experiments on LongBench demonstrate that DASH-KV significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baseline methods while matching the performance of full attention, all while reducing inference complexity from O(N^2) to linear O(N). The code is available at https://github.com/Zhihan-Zh/DASH-KV

2. RetrievalAttention: Accelerating Long-Context LLM Inference via Vector Retrieval (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 104)
   Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) have become increasingly important. However, due to the quadratic time complexity of attention computation, scaling LLMs to longer contexts incurs extremely slow inference speed and high GPU memory consumption for caching key-value (KV) vectors. This paper proposes RetrievalAttention, a training-free approach to both accelerate attention computation and reduce GPU memory consumption. By leveraging the dynamic sparsity of attention mechanism, RetrievalAttention proposes to build approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS) indexes for KV vectors in CPU memory and retrieve the most relevant ones through vector search during generation. Unfortunately, we observe that the off-the-shelf ANNS indexes are often ineffective for such retrieval tasks due to the out-of-distribution (OOD) between query vectors and key vectors in the attention mechanism. RetrievalAttention addresses the OOD challenge by designing an attention-aware vector search algorithm that can adapt to the distribution of query vectors. Our evaluation demonstrates that RetrievalAttention achieves near full attention accuracy while only requiring access to 1--3% of the data. This leads to a significant reduction in the inference cost of long-context LLMs, with a much lower GPU memory footprint. In particular, RetrievalAttention only needs a single NVIDIA RTX4090 (24GB) to serve 128K tokens for LLMs with 8B parameters, which is capable of generating one token in 0.188 seconds.

3. A Training-Free Sub-quadratic Cost Transformer Model Serving Framework with Hierarchically Pruned Attention (International Conference on Learning Representations 2024, cites: 9)
   In modern large language models (LLMs), increasing the context length is crucial for improving comprehension and coherence in long-context, multi-modal, and retrieval-augmented language generation. While many recent transformer models attempt to extend their context length over a million tokens, they remain impractical due to the quadratic time and space complexities. Although recent works on linear and sparse attention mechanisms can achieve this goal, their real-world applicability is often limited by the need to re-train from scratch and significantly worse performance. In response, we propose a novel approach, Hierarchically Pruned Attention (HiP), which reduces the time complexity of the attention mechanism to $O(T \log T)$ and the space complexity to $O(T)$, where $T$ is the sequence length. We notice a pattern in the attention scores of pretrained LLMs where tokens close together tend to have similar scores, which we call ``attention locality''. Based on this observation, we utilize a novel tree-search-like algorithm that estimates the top-$k$ key tokens for a given query on the fly, which is mathematically guaranteed to have better performance than random attention pruning. In addition to improving the time complexity of the attention mechanism, we further optimize GPU memory usage by implementing KV cache offloading, which stores only $O(\log T)$ tokens on the GPU while maintaining similar decoding throughput. Experiments on benchmarks show that HiP, with its training-free nature, significantly reduces both prefill and decoding latencies, as well as memory usage, while maintaining high-quality generation with minimal degradation. HiP enables pretrained LLMs to scale up to millions of tokens on commodity GPUs, potentially unlocking long-context LLM applications previously deemed infeasible.

4. Prefill-as-a-Service: KVCache of Next-Generation Models Could Go Cross-Datacenter ( 2026, cites: 2)
   Prefill-decode (PD) disaggregation has become the standard architecture for large-scale LLM serving, but in practice its deployment boundary is still determined by KVCache transfer. In conventional dense-attention models, prefill generates huge KVCache traffics that keep prefill and decode tightly coupled within a single high-bandwidth network domain, limiting heterogeneous deployment and resource elasticity. Recent hybrid-attention architectures substantially reduce KVCache size, making cross-cluster KVCache transport increasingly plausible. However, smaller KVCache alone does not make heterogeneous cross-datacenter PD serving practical: real workloads remain bursty, request lengths are highly skewed, prefix caches are unevenly distributed, and inter-cluster bandwidth fluctuates. A naive design that fully externalizes prefill can therefore still suffer from congestion, unstable queueing, and poor utilization. We present Prefill-as-a-Service (PrfaaS), a cross-datacenter serving architecture that selectively offloads long-context prefill to standalone, compute-dense prefill clusters and transfers the resulting KVCache over commodity Ethernet to local PD clusters for decode. Rather than treating reduced KVCache as sufficient, PrfaaS combines model-side KV efficiency with system-side selective offloading, bandwidth-aware scheduling, and cache-aware request placement. This design removes the requirement that heterogeneous accelerators share the same low-latency RDMA fabric, enabling independent scaling of prefill and decode capacity across loosely coupled clusters. In a case study using an internal 1T-parameter hybrid model, a PrfaaS-augmented heterogeneous deployment achieves 54% higher serving throughput and 64% lower P90 TTFT than a homogeneous PD baseline, with approximately 15% throughput gain at equal cost, while consuming only modest cross-datacenter bandwidth.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Long-context LLMs accumulate large KV caches that dominate
memory bandwidth; how can we compress or selectively evict
cache entries while preserving the model's ability to attend
to distant relevant tokens?


### Proposed method
We propose Predictive Hierarchical KV (PHKV), a three-part caching system that keeps exact recent context, compresses the distant past into a small set of multi-aspect summary slots per chunk, and recalls exact keys/values on demand via a lightweight predictor and an ANN index. At runtime, the model holds a full-fidelity sliding window (e.g., last W tokens) in DRAM. Older tokens are grouped into fixed-size chunks; each chunk is replaced by k summary KV slots that approximate the chunk’s attention behavior. A tiny predictor, queried with the current query vector Q_t and cheap metadata, decides which summaries (and, when needed, which exact evicted tokens) to materialize for attention at this step. Evicted exact KV entries are quantized and parked off-DRAM together with an ANN index over their keys to enable fast recall when Q_t demands it.

Chunk compression is multi-view and hierarchical. For each aging chunk, we cluster or low-rank factorize its key matrix to produce k centroids that act like viewer tokens capturing different semantic aspects of the chunk; corresponding summary values are fitted (e.g., via attention-weighted regression) so that attending to these k slots approximates attending to the full chunk. As chunks age further, we pyramid-merge summaries across adjacent chunks (k -> k/2) to cap memory growth while preserving a coarse global signal. Summaries retain position encodings (e.g., via centroided rotary phases) so relative alignment is not lost, and all summaries and their small per-slot stats (token counts, last-hit time) remain resident; full KVs are evicted.

Eviction and recall are predictor-guided to save bandwidth. A small MLP or logistic head takes features such as dot-products between Q_t and each summary key, recency, usage, and entropy of past attentions to predict which summaries will receive nontrivial attention mass. Only predicted-relevant summaries are exposed to the attention kernel. If a chosen summary’s pre-softmax score crosses a threshold, we probe the off-DRAM ANN index with Q_t (or the summary key) to fetch the top-r exact KVs from the corresponding chunk and transiently splice them into the attention computation, replacing or augmenting the coarse slot. This prefetch is quantized and batched across layers to amortize I/O. A strict bandwidth budget governs how many exact KVs can be pulled per step; when exceeded, we fall back to the summaries (graceful degradation).

Training makes the model robust to partial and summarized caches and teaches the predictor what to keep. We fine-tune with KV-dropout that randomly replaces old chunks by their k-slot summaries (and zero-pads missing entries) to match target retention rates, so the decoder learns to operate when only summaries are present. In parallel, we generate pseudo-relevance labels for chunks: for a small subset of steps during fine-tuning, we measure the change in next-token loss when including vs excluding a chunk (or its exact top-r tokens) and train the predictor to classify chunks as helpful or not; simple cosine-similarity gates with Q_t serve as a cheap proxy feature during inference. Distillation from full-cache teacher runs can further shape the summary fitting and predictor thresholds. At deployment, PHKV delivers: exact KVs for the recent window; k-per-chunk summaries for the distant past; a predictor that minimizes unnecessary memory traffic; and an ANN-backed, on-the-fly recall path that restores fidelity only where the model would otherwise under-attend to distant but relevant tokens.

### Rationale
Long contexts overload DRAM with large KV caches and saturate bandwidth, yet uniformly truncating or naively compressing distant tokens harms the ability to retrieve far-away facts. PHKV cuts bandwidth by (a) pyramiding older tokens into a tiny number of multi-aspect summaries, so the attention kernel rarely touches large blocks of KVs, and (b) using a learned predictor to avoid moving data that will not affect the softmax—only when the model is likely to need specificity do we fetch a small set of exact KVs. This preserves recall for distant evidence because every chunk remains represented by semantically diverse keys, and exact tokens can be reinstated on demand within a strict bandwidth budget.

Robustness comes from aligning training and inference: the model is explicitly exposed to partial/summarized caches and learns to treat missing tokens as zero-padded while relying on summaries, eliminating the distribution shift that typically degrades performance under cache thinning. Pseudo-relevance supervision and similarity-based gating provide a practical signal for what to keep, while the ANN index ensures that even if a useful token was evicted, it remains recoverable with low overhead. Together, these mechanisms maintain long-range attention quality while substantially reducing memory movement.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] LightPROF: A Lightweight Reasoning Framework for Large Language Model on Knowledge Graph (2504.03137)
   problem: Updating or fine-tuning LLMs to incorporate up-to-date KG knowledge is costly and risks catastrophic forgetting; there is a need for a parameter-efficient way to inject external KG knowledge into diverse LLMs.
   method:  LightPROF freezes the LLM and performs mixed prompting by inserting the learned knowledge soft prompts into carefully designed natural-language hard prompts in a chat-style template. Training optimizes next-token likelihood while only tuning the Knowledge Adapter (KnowledgeEncoder + Projector), making the approach plug-and-play for any open-source LLM that accepts token embeddings. Empirically, integrating LightPROF with small LLMs (e.g., LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA3-8B) yields strong KGQA performance and efficiency, demonstrating broad compatibility and parameter efficiency.

2. [LLM_NLP] LooGLE: Can Long-Context Language Models Understand Long Contexts? (2311.04939)
   problem: Existing long-context LLM benchmarks are limited by relatively short documents, potential data leakage from outdated sources, and tasks that mostly test short-range dependencies rather than true long-range reasoning across a document.
   method:  Introduce LooGLE, a long-context, generic evaluation benchmark built from post-2022 documents across arXiv papers, Wikipedia, and movie/TV scripts. It includes 776 extra-long documents (avg. ~19.3k words; many >100k words), 6,448 test instances, and 1,101 manually crafted, cross-validated long-dependency QA pairs spanning four challenging types (multiple information retrieval, timeline reorder, computation, and comprehension/reasoning), plus short-dependency QA, cloze, and summarization. The dataset construction, prompts, and evaluation code are open-sourced to enable reproducibility and periodic updates.

3. [LLM_NLP] LLM in a Flash: Efficient Large Language Model Inference with Limited Memory (2312.11514)
   problem: On-device LLM inference is constrained when model parameters exceed available DRAM; naively reloading large weights from flash each token is dominated by flash bandwidth/latency, making it too slow.
   method:  Store full model weights on flash and keep only embeddings and attention weights resident in DRAM to minimize repeated transfers. Exploit FFN activation sparsity by training a small low-rank predictor that, using only the current layer’s attention outputs, anticipates which intermediate neurons will be active after ReLU; then load only the corresponding FFN up/down weights from flash. This selective persistence and predictor-guided loading substantially reduces flash→DRAM traffic with marginal impact on zero-shot accuracy. (Evidence: selective persistence and sparsity use: lines 74–79, 76–77, predictor design and training: line 80, accuracy impact: lines 88–97.)

4. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

5. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

6. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Equipping text-pretrained LMs with vision-and-language capabilities efficiently—without appending image tokens that consume input length or full-model fine-tuning—requires a new mechanism to inject visual information with few parameters.
   method:  VL-HyperPELT treats image features as inputs to hypernetworks rather than as sequence tokens: CLIP-extracted visual features are projected into visual hyper-embeddings, which drive visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific prefix vectors for attention and adapter weights for FFN. These are combined with task-specific outputs (concatenating prefix vectors; parallel adapters) to modulate the frozen T5 while preserving the original text input length. This approach achieves comparable or better performance than VL-Adapter with fewer trainable parameters (6.62% vs 7.16%) and yields stronger few-shot transfer on OKVQA and SNLI-VE.

7. [CV] LeViT: A Vision Transformer in ConvNet's Clothing for Faster Inference (2104.01136)
   problem: Vision Transformers keep a fixed spatial resolution across layers, leading to high compute and suboptimal speed–accuracy trade-offs, especially on CPUs and edge hardware.
   method:  Introduce a multi-stage, pyramid Vision Transformer where resolution is progressively reduced via shrinking attention blocks placed between stages. These blocks subsample before Q, mapping (C,H,W) to (C',H/2,W/2), omit residual connections at downsampling, and set the number of heads to N=C/D to preserve information. This design shifts more compute to small activation maps and yields superior throughput at similar accuracy—for example, LeViT-256 matches EfficientNet-B3 at 81.6% ImageNet top-1 while being ~7× faster on an Intel CPU (42.5 vs 5.9 images/s).

8. [MULTIMODAL] Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers (2410.05800)
   problem: Vision transformers fine-tuned on partial token inputs (as stored in token-level memories) exhibit large performance drops because they are pre-trained to expect full-length token sequences.
   method:  Make transformers robust to partial inputs by always feeding full-length sequences where non-retained tokens are zero-padded to preserve positions, and by applying input token dropout on newly observed full samples during training to match the target retention rate. During replay, use stored token indices from the core tokenset to place tokens and pad the rest with zeros. This alignment of training and replay conditions eliminates the degradation from partial inputs and yields large gains in continual settings (e.g., avoiding ~15% drop at 30% retention and achieving ~40% accuracy improvement versus naive partial-input fine-tuning).

9. [CV] Fairness-aware Vision Transformer via Debiased Self-Attention (2301.13803)
   problem: Vision Transformers often exploit spurious correlations tied to sensitive attributes (e.g., gender-related cues when predicting hair color). Automatically locating and suppressing such spurious visual cues at the patch level without manual annotation is challenging.
   method:  DSA first trains a bias-only ViT with an adversarial objective L_B = L_S(x,s) − L_T(x,y), using gradient reversal, so the feature extractor learns to rely on spurious features predictive of the sensitive label s while failing on the target y. It then crafts attention-aware, patch-level adversarial examples against this bias-only model to localize and perturb top-k spurious patches: per-patch importance t_j^(l) is computed from attention distributions to select influential patches for s; an attention-aware loss L_Attn = Σ_{h,i} a_p^(l,h,i) is maximized to draw attention to the attacked patch; perturbations are optimized by combining sensitive-label CE and L_Attn with PCGrad to avoid gradient conflicts and PGD/Adam updates. The resulting adversarial examples x′ mask spurious patches and augment training data.

10. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

11. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. 1. HashEvict: A Pre-Attention KV Cache Eviction Strategy using Locality-Sensitive Hashing (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 9)
   Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) use the key-value (KV) cache to significantly accelerate inference by storing the key and value embeddings of past tokens. However, this cache consumes significant GPU memory. In this work, we introduce HashEvict, an algorithm that uses locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) to compress the KV cache. HashEvict quickly locates tokens in the cache that are cosine dissimilar to the current query token. This is achieved by computing the Hamming distance between binarized Gaussian projections of the current token query and cached token keys, with a projection length much smaller than the embedding dimension. We maintain a lightweight binary structure in GPU memory to facilitate these calculations. Unlike existing compression strategies that compute attention to determine token retention, HashEvict makes these decisions pre-attention, thereby reducing computational costs. Additionally, HashEvict is dynamic - at every decoding step, the key and value of the current token replace the embeddings of a token expected to produce the lowest attention score. We demonstrate that HashEvict can compress the KV cache by 30%-70% while maintaining high performance across reasoning, multiple-choice, long-context retrieval and summarization tasks.

Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Recent hybrid-attention architectures substantially reduce KVCache size, making cross-cluster KVCache transport increasingly plausible. However, smaller KVCache alone does not make heterogeneous cross-datacenter PD serving practical: real workloads remain bursty, request lengths are highly skewed, prefix caches are unevenly distributed, and inter-cluster bandwidth fluctuates. A naive design that fully externalizes prefill can therefore still suffer from congestion, unstable queueing, and poor utilization. We present Prefill-as-a-Service (PrfaaS), a cross-datacenter serving architecture that selectively offloads long-context prefill to standalone, compute-dense prefill clusters and transfers the resulting KVCache over commodity Ethernet to local PD clusters for decode. Rather than treating reduced KVCache as sufficient, PrfaaS combines model-side KV efficiency with system-side selective offloading, bandwidth-aware scheduling, and cache-aware request placement. This design removes the requirement that heterogeneous accelerators share the same low-latency RDMA fabric, enabling independent scaling of prefill and decode capacity across loosely coupled clusters. In a case study using an internal 1T-parameter hybrid model, a PrfaaS-augmented heterogeneous deployment achieves 54% higher serving throughput and 64% lower P90 TTFT than a homogeneous PD baseline, with approximately 15% throughput gain at equal cost, while consuming only modest cross-datacenter bandwidth.

1. More Than a Quick Glance: Overcoming the Greedy Bias in KV-Cache Compression (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   While Large Language Models (LLMs) can theoretically support extensive context windows, their actual deployment is constrained by the linear growth of Key-Value (KV) cache memory. Prevailing compression strategies mitigate this through various pruning mechanisms, yet trade-off semantic recall for memory efficiency. In this work, we present LASER-KV (Layer Accumulated Selection with Exact-LSH Recall), a framework designed to test the limits of KV compression under a strict accumulative budgeting policy. We deviate from the standard fixed summary size approach by implementing a block-wise accumulation strategy governed by a protection divisor (n). This allows us to isolate the effects of compression from sliding window artifacts. Our experiments on the Babilong benchmark reveal performance degradation in previous compression methods by 15-30% on various long context tasks. LASER-KV maintains stable performance, achieving superior accuracies by a margin of upto 10% at 128k. These findings challenge the prevailing assumption that attention scores alone are a sufficient proxy for token utility.

2. Near-Lossless Model Compression Enables Longer Context Inference in DNA Large Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Trained on massive cross-species DNA corpora, DNA large language models (LLMs) learn the fundamental"grammar"and evolutionary patterns of genomic sequences. This makes them powerful priors for DNA sequence modeling, particularly over long ranges. However, two major constraints hinder their use in practice: the quadratic computational cost of self-attention and the growing memory required for key-value (KV) caches during autoregressive decoding. These constraints force the use of heuristics such as fixed-window truncation or sliding windows, which compromise fidelity on ultra-long sequences by discarding distant information. We introduce FOCUS (Feature-Oriented Compression for Ultra-long Self-attention), a progressive context-compression module that can be plugged into pretrained DNA LLMs. FOCUS combines the established k-mer representation in genomics with learnable hierarchical compression: it inserts summary tokens at k-mer granularity and progressively compresses attention key and value activations across multiple Transformer layers, retaining only the summary KV states across windows while discarding ordinary-token KV. A shared-boundary windowing scheme yields a stationary cross-window interface that propagates long-range information with minimal loss. We validate FOCUS on an Evo-2-based DNA LLM fine-tuned on GRCh38 chromosome 1 with self-supervised training and randomized compression schedules to promote robustness across compression ratios. On held-out human chromosomes, FOCUS achieves near-lossless fidelity: compressing a 1 kb context into only 10 summary tokens (about 100x) shifts the average per-nucleotide probability by only about 0.0004. Compared to a baseline without compression, FOCUS reduces KV-cache memory and converts effective inference scaling from O(N^2) to near-linear O(N), enabling about 100x longer inference windows on commodity GPUs with near-lossless fidelity.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Long-context LLMs accumulate large KV caches that dominate
memory bandwidth; how can we compress or selectively evict
cache entries while preserving the model's ability to attend
to distant relevant tokens?


### Proposed method
We propose Predictive Hierarchical KV (PHKV), a three-part caching system that keeps exact recent context, compresses the distant past into a small set of multi-aspect summary slots per chunk, and recalls exact keys/values on demand via a lightweight predictor and an ANN index. At runtime, the model holds a full-fidelity sliding window (e.g., last W tokens) in DRAM. Older tokens are grouped into fixed-size chunks; each chunk is replaced by k summary KV slots that approximate the chunk’s attention behavior. A tiny predictor, queried with the current query vector Q_t and cheap metadata, decides which summaries (and, when needed, which exact evicted tokens) to materialize for attention at this step. Evicted exact KV entries are quantized and parked off-DRAM together with an ANN index over their keys to enable fast recall when Q_t demands it.

Chunk compression is multi-view and hierarchical. For each aging chunk, we cluster or low-rank factorize its key matrix to produce k centroids that act like viewer tokens capturing different semantic aspects of the chunk; corresponding summary values are fitted (e.g., via attention-weighted regression) so that attending to these k slots approximates attending to the full chunk. As chunks age further, we pyramid-merge summaries across adjacent chunks (k -> k/2) to cap memory growth while preserving a coarse global signal. Summaries retain position encodings (e.g., via centroided rotary phases) so relative alignment is not lost, and all summaries and their small per-slot stats (token counts, last-hit time) remain resident; full KVs are evicted.

Eviction and recall are predictor-guided to save bandwidth. A small MLP or logistic head takes features such as dot-products between Q_t and each summary key, recency, usage, and entropy of past attentions to predict which summaries will receive nontrivial attention mass. Only predicted-relevant summaries are exposed to the attention kernel. If a chosen summary’s pre-softmax score crosses a threshold, we probe the off-DRAM ANN index with Q_t (or the summary key) to fetch the top-r exact KVs from the corresponding chunk and transiently splice them into the attention computation, replacing or augmenting the coarse slot. This prefetch is quantized and batched across layers to amortize I/O. A strict bandwidth budget governs how many exact KVs can be pulled per step; when exceeded, we fall back to the summaries (graceful degradation).

Training makes the model robust to partial and summarized caches and teaches the predictor what to keep. We fine-tune with KV-dropout that randomly replaces old chunks by their k-slot summaries (and zero-pads missing entries) to match target retention rates, so the decoder learns to operate when only summaries are present. In parallel, we generate pseudo-relevance labels for chunks: for a small subset of steps during fine-tuning, we measure the change in next-token loss when including vs excluding a chunk (or its exact top-r tokens) and train the predictor to classify chunks as helpful or not; simple cosine-similarity gates with Q_t serve as a cheap proxy feature during inference. Distillation from full-cache teacher runs can further shape the summary fitting and predictor thresholds. At deployment, PHKV delivers: exact KVs for the recent window; k-per-chunk summaries for the distant past; a predictor that minimizes unnecessary memory traffic; and an ANN-backed, on-the-fly recall path that restores fidelity only where the model would otherwise under-attend to distant but relevant tokens.

### Rationale
Long contexts overload DRAM with large KV caches and saturate bandwidth, yet uniformly truncating or naively compressing distant tokens harms the ability to retrieve far-away facts. PHKV cuts bandwidth by (a) pyramiding older tokens into a tiny number of multi-aspect summaries, so the attention kernel rarely touches large blocks of KVs, and (b) using a learned predictor to avoid moving data that will not affect the softmax—only when the model is likely to need specificity do we fetch a small set of exact KVs. This preserves recall for distant evidence because every chunk remains represented by semantically diverse keys, and exact tokens can be reinstated on demand within a strict bandwidth budget.

Robustness comes from aligning training and inference: the model is explicitly exposed to partial/summarized caches and learns to treat missing tokens as zero-padded while relying on summaries, eliminating the distribution shift that typically degrades performance under cache thinning. Pseudo-relevance supervision and similarity-based gating provide a practical signal for what to keep, while the ANN index ensures that even if a useful token was evicted, it remains recoverable with low overhead. Together, these mechanisms maintain long-range attention quality while substantially reducing memory movement.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] LightPROF: A Lightweight Reasoning Framework for Large Language Model on Knowledge Graph (2504.03137)
   problem: Updating or fine-tuning LLMs to incorporate up-to-date KG knowledge is costly and risks catastrophic forgetting; there is a need for a parameter-efficient way to inject external KG knowledge into diverse LLMs.
   method:  LightPROF freezes the LLM and performs mixed prompting by inserting the learned knowledge soft prompts into carefully designed natural-language hard prompts in a chat-style template. Training optimizes next-token likelihood while only tuning the Knowledge Adapter (KnowledgeEncoder + Projector), making the approach plug-and-play for any open-source LLM that accepts token embeddings. Empirically, integrating LightPROF with small LLMs (e.g., LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA3-8B) yields strong KGQA performance and efficiency, demonstrating broad compatibility and parameter efficiency.

2. [LLM_NLP] LooGLE: Can Long-Context Language Models Understand Long Contexts? (2311.04939)
   problem: Existing long-context LLM benchmarks are limited by relatively short documents, potential data leakage from outdated sources, and tasks that mostly test short-range dependencies rather than true long-range reasoning across a document.
   method:  Introduce LooGLE, a long-context, generic evaluation benchmark built from post-2022 documents across arXiv papers, Wikipedia, and movie/TV scripts. It includes 776 extra-long documents (avg. ~19.3k words; many >100k words), 6,448 test instances, and 1,101 manually crafted, cross-validated long-dependency QA pairs spanning four challenging types (multiple information retrieval, timeline reorder, computation, and comprehension/reasoning), plus short-dependency QA, cloze, and summarization. The dataset construction, prompts, and evaluation code are open-sourced to enable reproducibility and periodic updates.

3. [LLM_NLP] LLM in a Flash: Efficient Large Language Model Inference with Limited Memory (2312.11514)
   problem: On-device LLM inference is constrained when model parameters exceed available DRAM; naively reloading large weights from flash each token is dominated by flash bandwidth/latency, making it too slow.
   method:  Store full model weights on flash and keep only embeddings and attention weights resident in DRAM to minimize repeated transfers. Exploit FFN activation sparsity by training a small low-rank predictor that, using only the current layer’s attention outputs, anticipates which intermediate neurons will be active after ReLU; then load only the corresponding FFN up/down weights from flash. This selective persistence and predictor-guided loading substantially reduces flash→DRAM traffic with marginal impact on zero-shot accuracy. (Evidence: selective persistence and sparsity use: lines 74–79, 76–77, predictor design and training: line 80, accuracy impact: lines 88–97.)

4. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

5. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

6. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Equipping text-pretrained LMs with vision-and-language capabilities efficiently—without appending image tokens that consume input length or full-model fine-tuning—requires a new mechanism to inject visual information with few parameters.
   method:  VL-HyperPELT treats image features as inputs to hypernetworks rather than as sequence tokens: CLIP-extracted visual features are projected into visual hyper-embeddings, which drive visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific prefix vectors for attention and adapter weights for FFN. These are combined with task-specific outputs (concatenating prefix vectors; parallel adapters) to modulate the frozen T5 while preserving the original text input length. This approach achieves comparable or better performance than VL-Adapter with fewer trainable parameters (6.62% vs 7.16%) and yields stronger few-shot transfer on OKVQA and SNLI-VE.

7. [CV] LeViT: A Vision Transformer in ConvNet's Clothing for Faster Inference (2104.01136)
   problem: Vision Transformers keep a fixed spatial resolution across layers, leading to high compute and suboptimal speed–accuracy trade-offs, especially on CPUs and edge hardware.
   method:  Introduce a multi-stage, pyramid Vision Transformer where resolution is progressively reduced via shrinking attention blocks placed between stages. These blocks subsample before Q, mapping (C,H,W) to (C',H/2,W/2), omit residual connections at downsampling, and set the number of heads to N=C/D to preserve information. This design shifts more compute to small activation maps and yields superior throughput at similar accuracy—for example, LeViT-256 matches EfficientNet-B3 at 81.6% ImageNet top-1 while being ~7× faster on an Intel CPU (42.5 vs 5.9 images/s).

8. [MULTIMODAL] Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers (2410.05800)
   problem: Vision transformers fine-tuned on partial token inputs (as stored in token-level memories) exhibit large performance drops because they are pre-trained to expect full-length token sequences.
   method:  Make transformers robust to partial inputs by always feeding full-length sequences where non-retained tokens are zero-padded to preserve positions, and by applying input token dropout on newly observed full samples during training to match the target retention rate. During replay, use stored token indices from the core tokenset to place tokens and pad the rest with zeros. This alignment of training and replay conditions eliminates the degradation from partial inputs and yields large gains in continual settings (e.g., avoiding ~15% drop at 30% retention and achieving ~40% accuracy improvement versus naive partial-input fine-tuning).

9. [CV] Fairness-aware Vision Transformer via Debiased Self-Attention (2301.13803)
   problem: Vision Transformers often exploit spurious correlations tied to sensitive attributes (e.g., gender-related cues when predicting hair color). Automatically locating and suppressing such spurious visual cues at the patch level without manual annotation is challenging.
   method:  DSA first trains a bias-only ViT with an adversarial objective L_B = L_S(x,s) − L_T(x,y), using gradient reversal, so the feature extractor learns to rely on spurious features predictive of the sensitive label s while failing on the target y. It then crafts attention-aware, patch-level adversarial examples against this bias-only model to localize and perturb top-k spurious patches: per-patch importance t_j^(l) is computed from attention distributions to select influential patches for s; an attention-aware loss L_Attn = Σ_{h,i} a_p^(l,h,i) is maximized to draw attention to the attacked patch; perturbations are optimized by combining sensitive-label CE and L_Attn with PCGrad to avoid gradient conflicts and PGD/Adam updates. The resulting adversarial examples x′ mask spurious patches and augment training data.

10. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

11. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

12. [ROBOTICS] Scene-Intuitive Agent for Remote Embodied Visual Grounding (2103.12944)
   problem: Generating reliable navigation actions under high-level instructions requires integrating scene-level and object-level evidence over time, but standard recurrent policies have limited long-horizon memory and weak guidance from underspecified language.
   method:  Use a memory-augmented attentive action decoder that treats the pretrained grounding models as encoders and fuses them with an addressable scene memory. The decoder: (a) conditions the instruction on the current panorama via ViLEncoder (ViLBERT+BiLSTM) to produce grounded text features; (b) performs object-level referring for each navigable view with ViLPointer using top-k box features; (c) builds orientation-aware navigable-view representations; and (d) maintains a scene memory storing embedded observations and previous actions, processed by Transformer blocks to yield a memory-augmented state s_a_t for action prediction via attention. At inference, it fuses action logits with scene/object grounding scores (l_t + g_sg + g_og) to reduce search bias and shorten trajectories. Trained with mixed IL+RL losses, this design achieves new state-of-the-art results on REVERIE across seen/unseen splits.

13. [IR_REC] History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval (2401.16659)
   problem: Conversational dense retrieval often concatenates the entire conversation history, which is long and noisy, causing shortcut history dependency where the retriever over-relies on irrelevant past turns and neglects the current query.
   method:  HAConvDR introduces context-denoised query reformulation via pseudo relevance judgments (PRJs) of historical turns. For each prior turn, it tests whether concatenating the historical query and its ground-truth passage to the current query improves retrieval metrics; only turns that improve performance are labeled relevant. The reformulated query is constructed by concatenating the current query with only these selected (relevant) historical queries and passages, reducing noise while retaining useful context. This PRJ explicitly considers both historical queries and their passages, capturing intent more fully than query-only selection.

14. [IR_REC] Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval (2203.08372)
   problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
   method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

15. [IR_REC] Denoising Time Cycle Modeling for Recommendation (2402.02718)
   problem: User behavior histories contain many interactions irrelevant to a given target item, which act as noise and degrade time-cycle modeling and CTR prediction.
   method:  Introduce a gated filter unit that measures relevance between the target item embedding and each behavior’s item embedding via a cosine-based score, then applies a thresholded, piece-wise gating to produce weights f_k, setting irrelevant behaviors to zero. This denoised sequence is then used for time-cycle modeling, ensuring only target-related behaviors contribute. (Supported: gated filter unit with cos activation and threshold; zeroing out noise behaviors.)

16. [SPEECH] Retrieval Augmented Generation based context discovery for ASR (2509.19567)
   problem: Contextual ASR performance degrades on rare and out-of-vocabulary words, but automatically discovering the right context without modifying/fine-tuning the ASR model or relying on structured catalogs remains challenging.
   method:  Introduce CB-RAG: a model-agnostic, embedding-based retrieval method that builds a query from the concatenated transcripts of the previous k VAD-detected segments and encodes it with a MiniLM sentence transformer (all-MiniLM-L6-v2). It retrieves Top-N nearest words via cosine similarity in a FAISS index over a large, frozen vocabulary of 466k non-stopword entries (augmented with definitions when available), and passes these as context to a black-box contextual biasing ASR. Across TED-LIUMv3, Earnings21, and SPGISpeech, CB-RAG reduces WER by up to ~17% relative to no-context while keeping latency close to baseline (≈1.02–1.36×).

17. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
   method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

18. [SPEECH] End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering (2511.09282)
   problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
   method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

19. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

20. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)

1. HashEvict: A Pre-Attention KV Cache Eviction Strategy using Locality-Sensitive Hashing (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 9)
   Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) use the key-value (KV) cache to significantly accelerate inference by storing the key and value embeddings of past tokens. However, this cache consumes significant GPU memory. In this work, we introduce HashEvict, an algorithm that uses locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) to compress the KV cache. HashEvict quickly locates tokens in the cache that are cosine dissimilar to the current query token. This is achieved by computing the Hamming distance between binarized Gaussian projections of the current token query and cached token keys, with a projection length much smaller than the embedding dimension. We maintain a lightweight binary structure in GPU memory to facilitate these calculations. Unlike existing compression strategies that compute attention to determine token retention, HashEvict makes these decisions pre-attention, thereby reducing computational costs. Additionally, HashEvict is dynamic - at every decoding step, the key and value of the current token replace the embeddings of a token expected to produce the lowest attention score. We demonstrate that HashEvict can compress the KV cache by 30%-70% while maintaining high performance across reasoning, multiple-choice, long-context retrieval and summarization tasks.

   Memory and computation remain core bottlenecks in long-horizon LLM inference due to the quadratic cost of self-attention and the ever-growing key-value (KV) cache. Existing strategies for memory-bounded inference, such as quantization, offloading, or heuristic KV eviction, either incur high orchestration costs or rely on unreliable attention-based proxies of importance. We propose TRIM-KV, a novel approach that learns each token's intrinsic importance at creation time via a lightweight retention gate. Each gate predicts a scalar retention score that decays over time, reflecting the long-term utility of the token for a specific layer and head. Tokens with low scores are evicted when the memory budget is exceeded, ensuring that the cache always contains the most critical tokens. TRIM-KV is trained efficiently through distillation from a frozen LLM combined with a capacity loss, requiring only gate fine-tuning and adding negligible inference overhead. Across mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME24), procedural generation (LongProc), conversational long-memory benchmarks (LongMemEval), and long-context understanding (LongBenchV2 and SCBench), TRIM-KV consistently outperforms strong eviction and learnable retrieval baselines, especially in low-memory regimes. Remarkably, it even surpasses full-cache models in some settings, showing that selective retention can serve as a form of regularization, suppressing noise from uninformative tokens. Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Beyond efficiency, retention scores provide insights into layer- and head-specific roles, suggesting a new path toward LLM interpretability.

We notice a pattern in the attention scores of pretrained LLMs where tokens close together tend to have similar scores, which we call ``attention locality''. Based on this observation, we utilize a novel tree-search-like algorithm that estimates the top-$k$ key tokens for a given query on the fly, which is mathematically guaranteed to have better performance than random attention pruning. In addition to improving the time complexity of the attention mechanism, we further optimize GPU memory usage by implementing KV cache offloading, which stores only $O(\log T)$ tokens on the GPU while maintaining similar decoding throughput. Experiments on benchmarks show that HiP, with its training-free nature, significantly reduces both prefill and decoding latencies, as well as memory usage, while maintaining high-quality generation with minimal degradation.    Prefill-decode (PD) disaggregation has become the standard architecture for large-scale LLM serving, but in practice its deployment boundary is still determined by KVCache transfer. However, smaller KVCache alone does not make heterogeneous cross-datacenter PD serving practical: real workloads remain bursty, request lengths are highly skewed, prefix caches are unevenly distributed, and inter-cluster bandwidth fluctuates. Rather than treating reduced KVCache as sufficient, PrfaaS combines model-side KV efficiency with system-side selective offloading, bandwidth-aware scheduling, and cache-aware request placement. This design removes the requirement that heterogeneous accelerators share the same low-latency RDMA fabric, enabling independent scaling of prefill and decode capacity across loosely coupled clusters. In a case study using an internal 1T-parameter hybrid model, a PrfaaS-augmented heterogeneous deployment achieves 54% higher serving throughput and 64% lower P90 TTFT than a homogeneous PD baseline, with approximately 15% throughput gain at equal cost, while consuming only modest cross-datacenter bandwidth.

   We present Adaptive Soft Rolling KV Freeze with Entropy-Guided Recovery (ASR-KF-EGR), a training-free inference-time framework for efficient large language model generation. Our method introduces a reversible soft-freeze mechanism that temporarily suspends key-value (KV) updates for low-importance tokens identified within a sliding attention window. Unlike eviction-based approaches that permanently discard context, ASR-KF-EGR preserves all tokens in off-GPU storage and restores them on demand. We extend the framework with sublinear freeze scheduling, where freeze duration grows sublinearly with repeated low-importance detections, preventing over-aggressive compression. Preliminary experiments on LLaMA-3 8B demonstrate 55-67% reduction in active KV cache size while maintaining generation quality and passing needle-in-haystack retrieval tests. The method is architecture-agnostic, requires no fine-tuning, and provides a practical solution for memory-constrained deployment of long-context LLMs.

4. MAGE: All-[MASK] Block Already Knows Where to Look in Diffusion LLM (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Block diffusion LLMs are emerging as a promising next paradigm for language generation, but their use of KV caching makes memory access a dominant bottleneck in long-context settings. While dynamic sparse attention has been actively explored, existing methods designed for autoregressive LLMs rely on approximate importance estimation and perform poorly when adapted to block diffusion. This work identifies a key opportunity unique to block diffusion: attention at the first All-[MASK] denoising step reliably predicts important KV entries and budget requirements, enabling MAGE to perform a single exact attention pass per block and reuse it for training-free sparse denoising. Across long-context benchmarks including LongBench and Needle-in-a-Haystack, MAGE achieves near-lossless accuracy with a fraction of the KV budget while delivering up to 3-4x end-to-end speedup, consistently outperforming AR-oriented sparse attention baselines. A lightweight fine-tuning strategy further strengthens [MASK]-guided patterns with minimal cost, requiring only a few hours of training on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU for both 1.5B and 7B models.

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).
