This insight motivates a simple uniform budget allocation strategy, which we show empirically outperforms more complex layer-varying schemes. Building on this, we introduce GUI-KV, a plug-and-play KV cache compression method for GUI agents that requires no retraining. GUI-KV combines two novel techniques: (i) spatial saliency guidance, which augments attention scores with the L2 norm of hidden states to better preserve semantically important visual tokens, and (ii) temporal redundancy scoring, which projects previous frames'keys onto the current frame's key subspace to preferentially prune redundant history. Across standard GUI agent benchmarks and models, GUI-KV outperforms competitive KV compression baselines, closely matching full-cache accuracy at modest budgets. Notably, in a 5-screenshot setting on the AgentNetBench benchmark, GUI-KV reduces decoding FLOPs by 38.9% while increasing step accuracy by 4.1% over the full-cache baseline. These results demonstrate that exploiting GUI-specific redundancies enables efficient and reliable agent performance.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Long-context LLMs accumulate large KV caches that dominate
memory bandwidth; how can we compress or selectively evict
cache entries while preserving the model's ability to attend
to distant relevant tokens?


### Proposed method
Build a dual-scale memory for long-context decoding: (1) a recent-window cache that stores full-precision keys/values (K,V) for the last W tokens per head, and (2) a long-range bank organized into fixed-length segments (e.g., 512–2k tokens) where each segment holds a compact summary per head plus a small set of high-importance "support" tokens. At inference, attention over distant context first consults each segment’s summary prototypes; only if a prototype scores highly does the model optionally touch its associated support tokens. This keeps most memory bandwidth on the recent window and a tiny set of routed long-range entries.

Attach a lightweight relevance selector that runs alongside decoding to assign keep scores to tokens and segments. The selector uses cheap features available at decode time (position, per-head key norms, a down-projected query·key score, cumulative attention so far, and topic/entity cues) to predict each token’s future utility. Periodically (e.g., at segment boundaries or every M tokens), perform an O(K) refinement pass over the candidate set to retain—using adjacent local swaps to bubble up the top-K tokens/segments—and evict the rest. Before eviction, roll evicted tokens into the segment summary using an uncertainty-gated exponential moving average: if the selector (or an auxiliary uncertainty head) is unsure the summary already captures a token, its contribution is up-weighted or the token is temporarily kept as a support exemplar.

Compress long-range memory per head with a prototype low-rank representation. For keys, maintain a small dictionary of head-specific prototype directions (e.g., 16–64 per segment) and store each rolled-up token as a soft assignment to prototypes (or a quantized residual). For values, learn a head-specific projection to a low-dimensional subspace and accumulate summary coefficients there. Each segment thus provides: prototype keys, low-rank value coefficients, and a handful of support tokens (original K,V) for hard cases. At query time, attention first scores prototype keys; their weights linearly combine low-rank values to yield an approximate context vector. If the max prototype score or a confidence metric exceeds a threshold, the model optionally re-attends to the few linked support tokens to refine the result, amortizing bandwidth to rare, high-gain lookups.

Allocate memory budgets and compression granularities per head, since some heads are dominantly local while others carry global signals. Learn these budgets with a sparsity-regularized allocation during distillation: train a student that uses the compressed/evicted memory against a teacher with a full cache, minimizing next-token loss and matching teacher attentions on sampled heads/layers, plus a reconstruction loss for prototype attention vs true attention over distant tokens. A mild group-sparsity penalty on per-head long-range usage encourages specialization and yields stable head-wise budgets. Deploy with infrequent maintenance steps (at segment boundaries) to bound CPU/GPU cache traffic; most tokens are written once then either summarized or dropped, and most long-range attention touches only prototypes, cutting both storage and memory bandwidth while preserving access to genuinely relevant distant content.

### Rationale
The approach reduces bandwidth by (a) sharply limiting how many distant tokens remain in full precision, (b) routing most long-range attention through tiny per-segment prototype summaries, and (c) performing eviction and summary updates only at coarse intervals. At the same time, it preserves the ability to use faraway context by keeping a small, relevance-scored set of support tokens and enabling a two-step retrieval: cheap prototype attention followed by selective refinement when the summary indicates a strong match.

Key design choices make this practical: a learned lightweight selector avoids scanning all distant tokens at full cost; an O(K) adjacent-swap refinement pass maintains a high-quality top-K keep set without quadratic work; prototype-based, low-rank K/V summaries retain the principal long-range signals per head; uncertainty-gated EMA roll-ups prevent premature overcompression; and per-head budget specialization focuses capacity on the heads that actually exploit long-range cues. Distillation from a full-cache teacher aligns the compressed memory’s attentions and token predictions with the original model, yielding strong fidelity under tight memory budgets.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

8. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

11. [MULTIMODAL] Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning (2412.02071)
   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)
   problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
   method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

Thought: The proposed method combines a dual-scale KV cache (recent window + segmented long-range with prototype/low-rank summaries + support tokens), a learned relevance selector for eviction, uncertainty-gated EMA roll-ups, and per-head budget allocation with distillation. Let me first search for the core idea of hierarchical/dual-scale KV cache compression with prototype summaries for long-context LLMs.
Query: dual scale KV cache compression prototype summary long context LLM inference

Round 2/10.

Last search results:
1. EMPIRIC: Exploring Missing Pieces in KV Cache Compression for Reducing Computation, Storage, and Latency in Long-Context LLM Inference (ACM SIGOPS Operating Systems Review 2025, cites: 0)
   Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) heavily depend on the KV cache for efficient handling of long context sequences. However, the size of the KV cache grows linearly with the input sequence length, increasingly straining system memory, computational resources, bandwidth, and latency during decoding. Although recent research has proposed various techniques to compress the KV cache -targeting either storage or computational efficiency-few methods effectively achieve both simultaneously. Additionally, existing methods primarily rely on heuristic-driven approaches, lacking comprehensive insights into token selection criteria, and often significantly compromise model accuracy under strict KV cache token budget constraints (e.g., keeping 512 tokens). Building upon our recent work, RocketKV, this paper introduces EMPIRIC as an oracle-based vision study, which explicitly defines theoretical bounds for accuracy, computation, and storage in KV cache compression. By analyzing intrinsic patterns in KV cache attention heads, EMPIRIC provides novel insights into effective token pruning without accuracy degradation. This work clarifies the overlooked elements critical to KV cache compression during decoding and optimally balances computational efficiency, storage optimization, inference latency, and accuracy. We envision that EMPIRIC will guide future research efforts toward creating scalable, efficient KV cache compression techniques, significantly improving inference performance for long context LLM inference.

2. MILLION: MasterIng Long-Context LLM Inference Via Outlier-Immunized KV Product QuaNtization (Design Automation Conference 2025, cites: 6)
   Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly utilized for complex tasks requiring longer context lengths, with some models supporting up to 128 K or 1 M tokens. This trend, however, presents significant challenges in inference speed and memory management. The primary bottleneck in long-context LLM inference is the quadratic computational complexity of attention mechanisms, causing substantial slowdowns as sequence length increases. KV cache mechanism alleviates this issue by storing pre-computed data, but introduces memory requirements that scale linearly with context length, hindering efficient LLM deployment. Quantization emerges as a promising approach to address the widening gap between LLM size and memory capacity. However, traditional quantization schemes often yield suboptimal compression results for KV caches due to two key factors: i) On-the-fly quantization and de-quantization, causing significant performance overhead; ii) Prevalence of outliers in KV values, challenging low-bitwidth uniform quantization. To this end, we propose MILLION, a novel quantization framework achieving low-bitwidth KV cache through product quantization. First, we conduct a thorough analysis of KV cache distribution, revealing the limitations of existing quantization schemes. Second, we introduce a non-uniform quantization algorithm based on product quantization, which efficiently compresses data while preserving accuracy. Third, we develop a high-performance GPU inference framework with efficient attention kernel and pipeline design for MILLION that leverages sparse computation and asynchronous quantization, significantly enhancing inference speed. Comprehensive evaluation results demonstrate that MILLION can achieve 4 bits quantization with trivial perplexity and accuracy loss, and achieve 2.09 x end-to-end performance gains at 32 K context length. Code is released at https://github.com/ZongwuWang/MILLION.

3. SmallKV: Small Model Assisted Compensation of KV Cache Compression for Efficient LLM Inference (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   KV cache eviction has emerged as an effective solution to alleviate resource constraints faced by LLMs in long-context scenarios. 1. Cache What Lasts: Token Retention for Memory-Bounded KV Cache in LLMs (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 5)

Across mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME24), procedural generation (LongProc), conversational long-memory benchmarks (LongMemEval), and long-context understanding (LongBenchV2 and SCBench), TRIM-KV consistently outperforms strong eviction and learnable retrieval baselines, especially in low-memory regimes. Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Compared to naive baselines like Sliding Window, TokenSkipping achieves better coherence under similar memory budgets and avoids global scoring, offering a simple, high-throughput optimization for memory-constrained long-context deployments.

TeleMem further introduces a structured writing pipeline that batches, retrieves, clusters, and consolidates memory entries, substantially improving storage efficiency, reducing token usage, and accelerating memory operations. messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Long-context LLMs accumulate large KV caches that dominate
memory bandwidth; how can we compress or selectively evict
cache entries while preserving the model's ability to attend
to distant relevant tokens?


### Proposed method
Build a dual-scale memory for long-context decoding: (1) a recent-window cache that stores full-precision keys/values (K,V) for the last W tokens per head, and (2) a long-range bank organized into fixed-length segments (e.g., 512–2k tokens) where each segment holds a compact summary per head plus a small set of high-importance "support" tokens. At inference, attention over distant context first consults each segment’s summary prototypes; only if a prototype scores highly does the model optionally touch its associated support tokens. This keeps most memory bandwidth on the recent window and a tiny set of routed long-range entries.

Attach a lightweight relevance selector that runs alongside decoding to assign keep scores to tokens and segments. The selector uses cheap features available at decode time (position, per-head key norms, a down-projected query·key score, cumulative attention so far, and topic/entity cues) to predict each token’s future utility. Periodically (e.g., at segment boundaries or every M tokens), perform an O(K) refinement pass over the candidate set to retain—using adjacent local swaps to bubble up the top-K tokens/segments—and evict the rest. Before eviction, roll evicted tokens into the segment summary using an uncertainty-gated exponential moving average: if the selector (or an auxiliary uncertainty head) is unsure the summary already captures a token, its contribution is up-weighted or the token is temporarily kept as a support exemplar.

Compress long-range memory per head with a prototype low-rank representation. For keys, maintain a small dictionary of head-specific prototype directions (e.g., 16–64 per segment) and store each rolled-up token as a soft assignment to prototypes (or a quantized residual). For values, learn a head-specific projection to a low-dimensional subspace and accumulate summary coefficients there. Each segment thus provides: prototype keys, low-rank value coefficients, and a handful of support tokens (original K,V) for hard cases. At query time, attention first scores prototype keys; their weights linearly combine low-rank values to yield an approximate context vector. If the max prototype score or a confidence metric exceeds a threshold, the model optionally re-attends to the few linked support tokens to refine the result, amortizing bandwidth to rare, high-gain lookups.

Allocate memory budgets and compression granularities per head, since some heads are dominantly local while others carry global signals. Learn these budgets with a sparsity-regularized allocation during distillation: train a student that uses the compressed/evicted memory against a teacher with a full cache, minimizing next-token loss and matching teacher attentions on sampled heads/layers, plus a reconstruction loss for prototype attention vs true attention over distant tokens. A mild group-sparsity penalty on per-head long-range usage encourages specialization and yields stable head-wise budgets. Deploy with infrequent maintenance steps (at segment boundaries) to bound CPU/GPU cache traffic; most tokens are written once then either summarized or dropped, and most long-range attention touches only prototypes, cutting both storage and memory bandwidth while preserving access to genuinely relevant distant content.

### Rationale
The approach reduces bandwidth by (a) sharply limiting how many distant tokens remain in full precision, (b) routing most long-range attention through tiny per-segment prototype summaries, and (c) performing eviction and summary updates only at coarse intervals. At the same time, it preserves the ability to use faraway context by keeping a small, relevance-scored set of support tokens and enabling a two-step retrieval: cheap prototype attention followed by selective refinement when the summary indicates a strong match.

Key design choices make this practical: a learned lightweight selector avoids scanning all distant tokens at full cost; an O(K) adjacent-swap refinement pass maintains a high-quality top-K keep set without quadratic work; prototype-based, low-rank K/V summaries retain the principal long-range signals per head; uncertainty-gated EMA roll-ups prevent premature overcompression; and per-head budget specialization focuses capacity on the heads that actually exploit long-range cues. Distillation from a full-cache teacher aligns the compressed memory’s attentions and token predictions with the original model, yielding strong fidelity under tight memory budgets.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.

1. EMPIRIC: Exploring Missing Pieces in KV Cache Compression for Reducing Computation, Storage, and Latency in Long-Context LLM Inference (ACM SIGOPS Operating Systems Review 2025, cites: 0)
   Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) heavily depend on the KV cache for efficient handling of long context sequences. However, the size of the KV cache grows linearly with the input sequence length, increasingly straining system memory, computational resources, bandwidth, and latency during decoding. Although recent research has proposed various techniques to compress the KV cache -targeting either storage or computational efficiency-few methods effectively achieve both simultaneously. Additionally, existing methods primarily rely on heuristic-driven approaches, lacking comprehensive insights into token selection criteria, and often significantly compromise model accuracy under strict KV cache token budget constraints (e.g., keeping 512 tokens). Building upon our recent work, RocketKV, this paper introduces EMPIRIC as an oracle-based vision study, which explicitly defines theoretical bounds for accuracy, computation, and storage in KV cache compression. By analyzing intrinsic patterns in KV cache attention heads, EMPIRIC provides novel insights into effective token pruning without accuracy degradation. This work clarifies the overlooked elements critical to KV cache compression during decoding and optimally balances computational efficiency, storage optimization, inference latency, and accuracy. We envision that EMPIRIC will guide future research efforts toward creating scalable, efficient KV cache compression techniques, significantly improving inference performance for long context LLM inference.

2. MILLION: MasterIng Long-Context LLM Inference Via Outlier-Immunized KV Product QuaNtization (Design Automation Conference 2025, cites: 6)
   Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly utilized for complex tasks requiring longer context lengths, with some models supporting up to 128 K or 1 M tokens. This trend, however, presents significant challenges in inference speed and memory management. The primary bottleneck in long-context LLM inference is the quadratic computational complexity of attention mechanisms, causing substantial slowdowns as sequence length increases. KV cache mechanism alleviates this issue by storing pre-computed data, but introduces memory requirements that scale linearly with context length, hindering efficient LLM deployment. Quantization emerges as a promising approach to address the widening gap between LLM size and memory capacity. However, traditional quantization schemes often yield suboptimal compression results for KV caches due to two key factors: i) On-the-fly quantization and de-quantization, causing significant performance overhead; ii) Prevalence of outliers in KV values, challenging low-bitwidth uniform quantization. To this end, we propose MILLION, a novel quantization framework achieving low-bitwidth KV cache through product quantization. First, we conduct a thorough analysis of KV cache distribution, revealing the limitations of existing quantization schemes. Second, we introduce a non-uniform quantization algorithm based on product quantization, which efficiently compresses data while preserving accuracy. Third, we develop a high-performance GPU inference framework with efficient attention kernel and pipeline design for MILLION that leverages sparse computation and asynchronous quantization, significantly enhancing inference speed. Comprehensive evaluation results demonstrate that MILLION can achieve 4 bits quantization with trivial perplexity and accuracy loss, and achieve 2.09 x end-to-end performance gains at 32 K context length. Code is released at https://github.com/ZongwuWang/MILLION.

3. SmallKV: Small Model Assisted Compensation of KV Cache Compression for Efficient LLM Inference (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   KV cache eviction has emerged as an effective solution to alleviate resource constraints faced by LLMs in long-context scenarios. However, existing token-level eviction methods often overlook two critical aspects: (1) their irreversible eviction strategy fails to adapt to dynamic attention patterns during decoding (the saliency shift problem), and (2) they treat both marginally important tokens and truly unimportant tokens equally, despite the collective significance of marginal tokens to model performance (the marginal information over-compression problem). To address these issues, we design two compensation mechanisms based on the high similarity of attention matrices between LLMs of different scales. We propose SmallKV, a small model assisted compensation method for KV cache compression. SmallKV can maintain attention matching between different-scale LLMs to: 1) assist the larger model in perceiving globally important information of attention; and 2) use the smaller model's attention scores to approximate those of marginal tokens in the larger model. Extensive experiments on benchmarks including GSM8K, BBH, MT-Bench, and LongBench demonstrate the effectiveness of SmallKV. Moreover, efficiency evaluations show that SmallKV achieves 1.75 - 2.56 times higher throughput than baseline methods, highlighting its potential for efficient and performant LLM inference in resource constrained environments.

   Large language models (LLMs) achieve state-of-the-art performance in many NLP tasks but incur prohibitive memory-access and compute costs when processing very long contexts due to linearly growing KV Cache. To address this trade-off, we propose SmartCache, a sparse inference system that exploits two-dimensional KV Cache similarity across adjacent decoding iterations and neighboring layers. We analyze the layer and iterative sparse patterns of KV Cache in long context LLM decoding and show that SmartCache maintains semantic consistency while drastically reducing redundant computation and memory traffic. Extensive experiments on Llama-3-8B-Instruct-Gradient-1048k, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-1M, and glm-4-9b-chat-1m across four long-context benchmarks report up to $\mathbf{3 0. 5 \%}$ end-to-end latency reduction and $\mathbf{1 5} \boldsymbol{\%} \mathbf{- 2 3 \%}$ average latency reduction, with inference accuracy degradation constrained within 2% and occasional slight improvements.    Recently, large language models (LLMs) have achieved huge success in the natural language processing (NLP) field, driving a growing demand to extend their deployment from the cloud to edge devices. At the algorithmic level, we integrate (1) pruning, (2) {\Lambda}-shaped attention, and (3) an innovative W2A8KV4 (2-bit weights, 8-bit activations, and 4-bit KV cache) quantization scheme, thus effectively reducing memory and bandwidth requirements while facilitating LLMs' long-sequence generation. At the hardware level, we design a dedicated FPGA-based accelerator with a reconfigurable computing engine to effectively and flexibly accommodate diverse operations arising from our compression algorithm, thereby fully translating the algorithmic innovations into tangible hardware efficiency. We validate AccLLM on the Xilinx Alveo U280 FPGA, demonstrating a 4.07x energy efficiency and a 2.98x throughput compared to the state-of-the-art work FlightLLM.

Across 330 experiments spanning two model families (Gemma 4 31B and Llama 3.1 70B), the fused sdpa_int4 kernel achieves 48x attention speedup at 128K context over the dequantize-then-attend baseline, reduces KV cache memory from 40 GB to 12.5 GB (3.2x compression), and maintains identical top-1 token predictions to FP16 inference.    Serving large language models (LLMs) at scale necessitates efficient key-value (KV) cache management. KV caches can be reused across conversation turns via shared-prefix prompts that are common in iterative code editing and chat. We present KVTC, a lightweight transform coder that compresses KV caches for compact on-GPU and off-GPU storage. Drawing on classical media compression, KVTC combines PCA-based feature decorrelation, adaptive quantization, and entropy coding. It requires only a brief initial calibration and leaves model parameters unchanged. By exploiting redundancies in KV caches, KVTC achieves up to 20$\times$ compression while maintaining reasoning and long-context accuracy, and 40$\times$ or higher for specific use cases. It consistently outperforms inference-time baselines such as token eviction, quantization, and SVD-based methods, while achieving higher compression ratios. These results support KVTC as a practical building block for memory-efficient LLM serving with reusable KV caches.

10. KV Cache Optimization Strategies for Scalable and Efficient LLM Inference ( 2026, cites: 0)
   The key-value (KV) cache is a foundational optimization in Transformer-based large language models (LLMs), eliminating redundant recomputation of past token representations during autoregressive generation. However, its memory footprint scales linearly with context length, imposing critical bottlenecks on GPU memory capacity, memory bandwidth, and inference throughput as production LLMs push context windows from thousands to millions of tokens. Efficient KV cache management has thus become a first-order challenge for scalable LLM deployment. This paper provides a systematic review of recent KV cache optimization techniques, organizing them into five principal directions: cache eviction, cache compression, hybrid memory solutions, novel attention mechanisms, and combination strategies. For each category we analyze the underlying mechanisms, deployment trade-offs, and empirical performance across memory reduction, throughput, and model accuracy metrics. We further map techniques to seven practical deployment scenarios, including long-context single requests, high-throughput datacenter serving, edge devices, multi-turn conversations, and accuracy-critical reasoning, providing actionable guidance for practitioners selecting among competing approaches. Our analysis reveals that no single technique dominates across all settings; instead, the optimal strategy depends on context length, hardware constraints, and workload characteristics, pointing toward adaptive, multi-stage optimization pipelines as a promising direction for future research.

1. Cache What Lasts: Token Retention for Memory-Bounded KV Cache in LLMs (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 5)
   Memory and computation remain core bottlenecks in long-horizon LLM inference due to the quadratic cost of self-attention and the ever-growing key-value (KV) cache. Existing strategies for memory-bounded inference, such as quantization, offloading, or heuristic KV eviction, either incur high orchestration costs or rely on unreliable attention-based proxies of importance. We propose TRIM-KV, a novel approach that learns each token's intrinsic importance at creation time via a lightweight retention gate. Each gate predicts a scalar retention score that decays over time, reflecting the long-term utility of the token for a specific layer and head. Tokens with low scores are evicted when the memory budget is exceeded, ensuring that the cache always contains the most critical tokens. TRIM-KV is trained efficiently through distillation from a frozen LLM combined with a capacity loss, requiring only gate fine-tuning and adding negligible inference overhead. Across mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME24), procedural generation (LongProc), conversational long-memory benchmarks (LongMemEval), and long-context understanding (LongBenchV2 and SCBench), TRIM-KV consistently outperforms strong eviction and learnable retrieval baselines, especially in low-memory regimes. Remarkably, it even surpasses full-cache models in some settings, showing that selective retention can serve as a form of regularization, suppressing noise from uninformative tokens. Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Beyond efficiency, retention scores provide insights into layer- and head-specific roles, suggesting a new path toward LLM interpretability.

2. Near-Oracle KV Selection via Pre-hoc Sparsity for Long-Context Inference (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   A core bottleneck in large language model (LLM) inference is the cost of attending over the ever-growing key-value (KV) cache. Although near-oracle top-k KV selection can preserve the quality of dense attention while sharply reducing computation and bandwidth, existing sparse methods generally rely on posterior heuristics, i.e., selectors conditioned on observed attention or proxy scores. Such conditioning introduces posterior bias: it tends to distort true token importance and miss salient tokens, thereby impairing long-range reasoning. To tackle this problem, we propose Pre-hoc Sparsity (PrHS), which selects KV entries before attention scoring and provides explicit accuracy control. Let the attention mass of discarded entries be delta (the dropped mass). Through a marginal-to-mutual-information analysis, we derive an upper bound on the mutual-information loss that depends only on the dropped mass. This relation explains failure modes of posterior heuristics and enables verifiable guarantees by controlling the dropped mass in advance. Each gate predicts a scalar retention score that decays over time, reflecting the long-term utility of the token for a specific layer and head. Tokens with low scores are evicted when the memory budget is exceeded, ensuring that the cache always contains the most critical tokens. TRIM-KV is trained efficiently through distillation from a frozen LLM combined with a capacity loss, requiring only gate fine-tuning and adding negligible inference overhead. Across mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME24), procedural generation (LongProc), conversational long-memory benchmarks (LongMemEval), and long-context understanding (LongBenchV2 and SCBench), TRIM-KV consistently outperforms strong eviction and learnable retrieval baselines, especially in low-memory regimes. Remarkably, it even surpasses full-cache models in some settings, showing that selective retention can serve as a form of regularization, suppressing noise from uninformative tokens. Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Beyond efficiency, retention scores provide insights into layer- and head-specific roles, suggesting a new path toward LLM interpretability.

10. GUI-KV: Efficient GUI Agents via KV Cache with Spatio-Temporal Awareness (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 3)
   Graphical user interface (GUI) agents built on vision-language models have emerged as a promising approach to automate human-computer workflows. However, they also face the inefficiency challenge as they process long sequences of high-resolution screenshots and solving long-horizon tasks, making inference slow, costly and memory-bound. Across standard GUI agent benchmarks and models, GUI-KV outperforms competitive KV compression baselines, closely matching full-cache accuracy at modest budgets. Notably, in a 5-screenshot setting on the AgentNetBench benchmark, GUI-KV reduces decoding FLOPs by 38.9% while increasing step accuracy by 4.1% over the full-cache baseline. These results demonstrate that exploiting GUI-specific redundancies enables efficient and reliable agent performance.

   Scaling large language models to long contexts is challenging due to the quadratic computational cost of full attention. Mitigation approaches include KV-cache selection or compression techniques. We instead provide an effective and end-to-end learnable bridge between the two without requiring architecture modification. In particular, our key insight is that interleaved gist compression tokens -- which provide a learnable summary of sets of raw tokens -- can serve as routing signals for sparse attention. Building on this, we introduce selective unfolding via GSA, which first compresses the context into gist tokens, then selects the most relevant gists, and subsequently restores the corresponding raw chunks for detailed attention. This yields a simple coarse-to-fine mechanism that combines compact global representations with targeted access to fine-grained evidence. We further incorporate this process directly into training in an end-to-end fashion, avoiding the need for external retrieval modules. In addition, we extend the framework hierarchically via recursive gist-of-gist construction, enabling multi-resolution context access with logarithmic per-step decoding complexity. Empirical results on LongBench and RAG benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms other compression baselines as well as inference-time sparse attention methods across compression ratios from $8\times$ to $32\times$. messages.content:
You are a rigorous research evaluator performing a PAIRWISE comparison
between two proposed methods for the same research query, on the
NOVELTY axis.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Comparison rules
- You see Method A and Method B side-by-side; their condition labels
  are hidden, and their order has been randomized for this comparison.
- Judge novelty by hypothetical literature precedent: which method is
  harder to surface in a Semantic Scholar search? Which has a more
  original combination of mechanisms or introduces a new named
  component / training signal?
- Compare the *strongest novel element* in each method. A method
  with one clearly novel mechanism beats a method with three
  well-established mechanisms.
- DO NOT reward verbosity or technical impressiveness; novelty is
  about non-existence in prior work, not about how detailed the
  method is.
- DO NOT reward problem-method fit (that is the validity axis).

## Output format (no markdown)
  Reasoning: <2-4 sentences identifying the strongest novel element
              in each method, then the comparison>
  Winner: <A | B | tie>

Use "tie" only when the methods are genuinely indistinguishable on
novelty; do not default to "tie" when uncertain.

## Research query
Long-context LLMs accumulate large KV caches that dominate
memory bandwidth; how can we compress or selectively evict
cache entries while preserving the model's ability to attend
to distant relevant tokens?


## Method A
Build a dual-scale memory for long-context decoding: (1) a recent-window cache that stores full-precision keys/values (K,V) for the last W tokens per head, and (2) a long-range bank organized into fixed-length segments (e.g., 512–2k tokens) where each segment holds a compact summary per head plus a small set of high-importance "support" tokens. At inference, attention over distant context first consults each segment’s summary prototypes; only if a prototype scores highly does the model optionally touch its associated support tokens. This keeps most memory bandwidth on the recent window and a tiny set of routed long-range entries.

Attach a lightweight relevance selector that runs alongside decoding to assign keep scores to tokens and segments. The selector uses cheap features available at decode time (position, per-head key norms, a down-projected query·key score, cumulative attention so far, and topic/entity cues) to predict each token’s future utility. Periodically (e.g., at segment boundaries or every M tokens), perform an O(K) refinement pass over the candidate set to retain—using adjacent local swaps to bubble up the top-K tokens/segments—and evict the rest. Before eviction, roll evicted tokens into the segment summary using an uncertainty-gated exponential moving average: if the selector (or an auxiliary uncertainty head) is unsure the summary already captures a token, its contribution is up-weighted or the token is temporarily kept as a support exemplar.

Compress long-range memory per head with a prototype low-rank representation. For keys, maintain a small dictionary of head-specific prototype directions (e.g., 16–64 per segment) and store each rolled-up token as a soft assignment to prototypes (or a quantized residual). For values, learn a head-specific projection to a low-dimensional subspace and accumulate summary coefficients there. Each segment thus provides: prototype keys, low-rank value coefficients, and a handful of support tokens (original K,V) for hard cases. At query time, attention first scores prototype keys; their weights linearly combine low-rank values to yield an approximate context vector. If the max prototype score or a confidence metric exceeds a threshold, the model optionally re-attends to the few linked support tokens to refine the result, amortizing bandwidth to rare, high-gain lookups.

Allocate memory budgets and compression granularities per head, since some heads are dominantly local while others carry global signals. Learn these budgets with a sparsity-regularized allocation during distillation: train a student that uses the compressed/evicted memory against a teacher with a full cache, minimizing next-token loss and matching teacher attentions on sampled heads/layers, plus a reconstruction loss for prototype attention vs true attention over distant tokens. A mild group-sparsity penalty on per-head long-range usage encourages specialization and yields stable head-wise budgets. Deploy with infrequent maintenance steps (at segment boundaries) to bound CPU/GPU cache traffic; most tokens are written once then either summarized or dropped, and most long-range attention touches only prototypes, cutting both storage and memory bandwidth while preserving access to genuinely relevant distant content.

## Method B
We propose Predictive Hierarchical KV (PHKV), a three-part caching system that keeps exact recent context, compresses the distant past into a small set of multi-aspect summary slots per chunk, and recalls exact keys/values on demand via a lightweight predictor and an ANN index. At runtime, the model holds a full-fidelity sliding window (e.g., last W tokens) in DRAM. Older tokens are grouped into fixed-size chunks; each chunk is replaced by k summary KV slots that approximate the chunk’s attention behavior. A tiny predictor, queried with the current query vector Q_t and cheap metadata, decides which summaries (and, when needed, which exact evicted tokens) to materialize for attention at this step. Evicted exact KV entries are quantized and parked off-DRAM together with an ANN index over their keys to enable fast recall when Q_t demands it.

Chunk compression is multi-view and hierarchical. For each aging chunk, we cluster or low-rank factorize its key matrix to produce k centroids that act like viewer tokens capturing different semantic aspects of the chunk; corresponding summary values are fitted (e.g., via attention-weighted regression) so that attending to these k slots approximates attending to the full chunk. As chunks age further, we pyramid-merge summaries across adjacent chunks (k -> k/2) to cap memory growth while preserving a coarse global signal. Summaries retain position encodings (e.g., via centroided rotary phases) so relative alignment is not lost, and all summaries and their small per-slot stats (token counts, last-hit time) remain resident; full KVs are evicted.

Eviction and recall are predictor-guided to save bandwidth. A small MLP or logistic head takes features such as dot-products between Q_t and each summary key, recency, usage, and entropy of past attentions to predict which summaries will receive nontrivial attention mass. Only predicted-relevant summaries are exposed to the attention kernel. If a chosen summary’s pre-softmax score crosses a threshold, we probe the off-DRAM ANN index with Q_t (or the summary key) to fetch the top-r exact KVs from the corresponding chunk and transiently splice them into the attention computation, replacing or augmenting the coarse slot. This prefetch is quantized and batched across layers to amortize I/O. A strict bandwidth budget governs how many exact KVs can be pulled per step; when exceeded, we fall back to the summaries (graceful degradation).

Training makes the model robust to partial and summarized caches and teaches the predictor what to keep. We fine-tune with KV-dropout that randomly replaces old chunks by their k-slot summaries (and zero-pads missing entries) to match target retention rates, so the decoder learns to operate when only summaries are present. In parallel, we generate pseudo-relevance labels for chunks: for a small subset of steps during fine-tuning, we measure the change in next-token loss when including vs excluding a chunk (or its exact top-r tokens) and train the predictor to classify chunks as helpful or not; simple cosine-similarity gates with Q_t serve as a cheap proxy feature during inference. Distillation from full-cache teacher runs can further shape the summary fitting and predictor thresholds. At deployment, PHKV delivers: exact KVs for the recent window; k-per-chunk summaries for the distant past; a predictor that minimizes unnecessary memory traffic; and an ANN-backed, on-the-fly recall path that restores fidelity only where the model would otherwise under-attend to distant but relevant tokens.

Which method is more NOVEL?

response:
Reasoning: Method A's strongest novel element is the uncertainty-gated exponential moving average for rolling evicted tokens into segment summaries, combined with per-head budget allocation learned via sparsity-regularized distillation. These are interesting but individually recognizable components (EMA summarization, head-wise specialization, distillation). Method B's strongest novel element is the ANN-backed on-demand exact KV recall path: evicted tokens are quantized and stored off-DRAM with an ANN index, and a lightweight predictor decides at each decoding step whether to fetch exact KVs back transiently and splice them into the attention computation. This "evict-but-recall-on-demand via ANN" mechanism—where the cache is not truly discarded but becomes a queryable external store with bandwidth-budgeted retrieval—is a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination than Method A's prototype/EMA approach, which more closely resembles existing streaming attention and token merging literature. The pyramid-merging of summaries across aging chunks also adds a hierarchical compression angle not present in Method A.

Winner: B

## Definition

Which method has higher VALIDITY (problem-method fit)?

response:
Reasoning: The failure mode is that large KV caches dominate memory bandwidth, causing models to lose access to distant relevant tokens when entries are evicted. Both methods address this directly: Method A uses dual-scale memory with prototype-based compression, selective eviction with uncertainty-gated summarization, and per-head budget allocation, with a traceable pathway from bandwidth reduction (prototypes instead of full KVs) to preserving distant relevant tokens (support tokens + prototype scoring). Method B uses chunk-based summarization, a predictor-guided recall system, and an ANN index for on-demand exact KV retrieval, with a traceable pathway from eviction (chunks replaced by summary slots) to preserving distant relevant tokens (predictor + ANN recall of exact KVs when needed). Both methods engage the failure mode head-on. However, Method B's ANN-backed exact recall path more directly addresses the specific concern of "preserving the model's ability to attend to distant relevant tokens" — it can restore full-fidelity KVs on demand rather than relying solely on approximate summaries. Method A's support tokens serve a similar role but are more constrained (fixed small set per segment). The two methods are comparably direct in their engagement with the failure mode overall.

Winner: tie

You are a rigorous research evaluator performing a PAIRWISE comparison
between two proposed methods for the same research query, on the
COHERENCE axis.

## Definition
Coherence = the method, as written, holds together as a technically
valid ML approach. A coherent method:
  - has internally consistent steps (no contradictions, no missing
    prerequisites);
  - combines its proposed mechanisms in compatible ways (not
    paradigm-mixing without explanation);
  - makes assumptions that are achievable given its stated inputs,
    models, and resources;
  - could plausibly be implemented by an ML practitioner without
    having to redesign or invent missing components.

Coherence is NOT:
- novelty (whether the combination is new — separate axis);
- validity (whether the mechanism addresses the query's failure
  mode — separate axis);
- effectiveness (whether the method would empirically win — out of
  scope);
- elegance or technical impressiveness.

## Comparison rules
- You see Method A and Method B side-by-side; their condition labels
  are hidden, and their order has been randomized for this comparison.
